reasoning: Candidate A better serves the full brief despite its weaker visual polish and Section 12 count gate failure. Its first viewport directly supports the primary business outcome with restaurant/date/party criteria, no-login trust, sample alert, and immediate free-alert action, so Segment A can act without scrolling while Segments B and C get explicit downstream routes. Candidate B has a cleaner, more composed first fold and lower visual trope risk, but it under-instantiates the travel-agent and segment-self-selection obligations, leans heavily toward Segment B, and has a worse programmatic coherence failure with missing and unjustified components. A also resolves the high-trust objection more concretely by placing the no-login badge, official handoff, sample alert, form, and risk reducers in the primary action zone. On mobile both preserve a visible CTA and trust copy, but A loses some product proof below fold while B has a simpler readable stack, so this is effectively tied. Overall, A is more likely to convert the stated mixed audience once its structural count issue is repaired.
best_reusable_move_a: The desktop hero combines actual alert setup fields with no-login trust, risk reducers, and a sample alert preview, making the product feel usable immediately rather than merely described.
best_reusable_move_b: The comparison-ready expert zone is well sequenced after the trust-first hero and gives Segment B concrete performance claims, notification channels, and tier value without putting dense tables above the fold.
